verbatim: [§1] But you are indulgent, and do not correct your family, but have allowed them to become corrupt. For this reason the Lord is angry with you, but he will heal all the past evils in your family, for because of their sins and wickednesses have you been corrupted by the things of daily life. [...] Do not cease, then, correcting your children, for I know that if they repent with all their heart, they will be inscribed in the books of life with the saints.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific societal and religious expectation placed upon parents to actively correct and discipline their children. Failure to do so is cited as a cause for divine anger, establishing the exercise of discipline as a necessary paternal/maternal function.

verbatim: [§1] But they who have evil designs in their hearts bring upon themselves death and captivity, especially those who obtain this world for themselves, and glory in their wealthy and do not lay hold of the good things which are to come. Their hearts will repent; yet have they no hope, but they have abandoned themselves and their life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage highlights that those who 'glory in their wealth' and focus on material acquisition are specifically at risk of losing 'the good things to come' and having 'no hope', effectively creating a group boundary based on wealth that limits access to spiritual resources.

verbatim: [§1] He who brought me up sold me to a certain Rhoda at Rome. [...] [§3] [1] He who brought me up sold me to a certain Rhoda at Rome.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly mentions being sold as property to a woman named Rhoda. While the text is a religious vision, the social reality of human sale (slavery) based on the status of the individual is the foundational context.

verbatim: [§17] But it is not for this that God is angry with you, but in order that you should convert your family, which has sinned against the Lord, and against you, their parents. But you are indulgent, and do not correct your family, but have allowed them to become corrupt. ... Do not cease, then, correcting your children, for I know that if they repent with all their heart, they will be inscribed in the books of life with the saints.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a social and religious rule where parents are responsible for the moral conduct of their children and describes the failure to provide 'correction' as a cause for divine anger.

verbatim: [§32] For the Master has sworn to his elect by his glory that if there be still sin after this day has been fixed, they shall find no salvation; for repentance for the just has an end; the days of repentance have been fulfilled for all the saints, but for the heathen repentance is open until the last day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a temporal boundary where members of the religious in-group (saints/just) lose the resource of repentance after a specific deadline, whereas the out-group (heathen) retains it longer.

verbatim: [§32] for repentance for the just has an end; the days of repentance have been fulfilled for all the saints, but for the heathen repentance is open until the last day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule explicitly grants a longer period for the 'heathen' group to repent compared to the 'saints', framing the time window as the resource.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive and read sacred texts
verbatim: [§26] The ancient lady came and asked me if I had already given the book to the elders. I said that I had not given it. You have done well, she said, for I have words to add. ... but in this city you shall read it yourself with the elders who are in charge of the church.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text directs that the authored work must be read specifically with the elders in charge, defining a specific group based on their occupation/role in the community who have access to the resource of the text.

verbatim: [§26] You shall therefore write two little books and send one to Clement and one to Grapte. Clement then shall send it to the cities abroad, for that is his duty; and Grapte shall exhort the widows and orphans; but in this city you shall read it yourself with the elders who are in charge of the church.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Grapte (a female name) is assigned a specific role of spiritual exhortation towards widows and orphans, distinguishing her role from the male leaders (Clement/Elders).

verbatim: [§37] For the Master has sworn to his elect by his glory that if there be still sin after this day has been fixed, they shall find no salvation; for repentance for the just has an end; the days of repentance have been fulfilled for all the saints, but for the heathen repentance is open until the last day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a clear distinction between the 'saints' (insiders) and the 'heathen' (outsiders) regarding the timeline of spiritual recourse. While the window for believers is closing, the out-group (heathen) retains the resource of repentance for a longer duration.

verbatim: [§37] For the Master has sworn to his elect by his glory that if there be still sin after this day has been fixed, they shall find no salvation; for repentance for the just has an end; the days of repentance have been fulfilled for all the saints, but for the heathen repentance is open until the last day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage imposes a specific temporal restriction on the community of believers (saints) that does not apply to the heathen, limiting their access to the resource of repentance after a fixed day.

extracted_rules.resource: Authority to communicate with foreign cities
verbatim: [§46] Clement then shall send it to the cities abroad, for that is his duty; and Grapte shall exhort the widows and orphans; but in this city you shall read it yourself with the elders who are in charge of the church.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines specific roles within the early church hierarchy. Clement is granted the specific immaterial resource/power of international communication and distribution of doctrine, a role distinguished from the local elders.

extracted_rules.resource: Authority to exhort widows and orphans
verbatim: [§46] Clement then shall send it to the cities abroad, for that is his duty; and Grapte shall exhort the widows and orphans; but in this city you shall read it yourself with the elders who are in charge of the church.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text assigns Grapte (a woman) the specific religious and communal resource of 'exhortation' targeting specific internal groups (widows/orphans), establishing her limited but defined sphere of influence.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to oversee local church readings
verbatim: [§49] but in this city you shall read it yourself with the elders who are in charge of the church.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies 'elders' as a specific functional group (defined by their occupation/office) who possess the authority to manage the reception and reading of sacred texts within their local jurisdiction.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to seat on the right hand
verbatim: [§50] The seat on the right is for others, who have already been found well-pleasing to God and have suffered for the Name. But you fall far short of sitting with them. [...] Listen, she said: Stripes, imprisonments, great afflictions, crucifixions, wild beasts, for the sake of the Name. Therefore is it given to them to be on the right hand of the Holiness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a clear social/ecclesial hierarchy where those who have physically suffered for their faith (martyrs/confessors) are granted a more honorable position (the right hand) compared to other believers.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for inclusion in the Church
verbatim: [§50] These are they which have faith, but have also the riches of this world. When persecution comes, because of their wealth and because of business they deny their Lord. [...] so too they who have riches in this world cannot be useful to the Lord unless their wealth be cut away from them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text uses the allegory of stones to show that the wealthy are excluded from the 'tower' (the Church) as long as they possess riches, as their business interests lead them to apostasy.

verbatim: [§50] The stones which are square and white and which fit into their joins are the Apostles and bishops and teachers and deacons who walked according to the majesty of God, and served the elect of God in holiness and reverence as bishops and teachers and deacons.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies specific occupational roles within the community (Apostles, bishops, deacons) that are foundational and perfectly integrated into the structure of the religious society.

verbatim: [§50] Now, therefore, listen to me and be at peace among yourselves, and regard one another and help one another, and do not take a superabundant share of the creatures of God for yourselves, but give also a part to those who lack. [...] So this lack of sharing is harmful to you who are rich, and do not share with the poor.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The lady (the Church) issues a community rule requiring those with 'over-abundance' to provide for the hungry, indicating a restriction on the right to keep personal wealth when others lack basic needs.

verbatim: [§50] I said to her: Lady, let the elders sit first. She said: Do what I tell you, and sit down.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hermas's invitation for the 'elders' to sit first reflects a standard social norm of age-based precedence which he attempts to follow even in the vision.

verbatim: [§59] [8] And after the young men had gone away and we were alone, she said to me: Sit here. I said to her: Lady, let the elders sit first. She said: Do what I tell you, and sit down.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hermas's automatic suggestion that the elders should sit before him reflects a standard social protocol of the time where seniority granted precedence in seating.

extracted_rules.group: those who suffered for the Name
verbatim: [§60] [9] ... The seat on the right is for others, who have already been found well-pleasing to God and have suffered for the Name. But you fall far short of sitting with them.
[§62] [1] ... Stripes, imprisonments, great afflictions, crucifixions, wild beasts, for the sake of the Name. Therefore is it given to them to be on the right hand of the Holiness...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a clear rule of hierarchy within the community's spiritual architecture: those who have physically suffered persecution (martyrs/confessors) are granted the superior resource of seating on the right hand, which carries more glory.

verbatim: [§92] [5] But who. Lady, are the white and round ones which do not fit into the building? She answered and said to me, How long will you be stupid and foolish, and ask everything and understand nothing? These are they which have faith, but have also the riches of this world. When persecution comes, because of their wealth and because of business they deny their Lord. [§93] [6] And I answered and said to her, Lady, but then when will they be useful for the building? When, she said, their wealth, which leads their souls astray, shall be cut off from them, then they will be useful to God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes an organizational rule where individuals possessing wealth are barred from 'fitting into the building' (the community) because their business interests lead to apostasy during persecution. Membership is only granted once the wealth is removed.

verbatim: [§81] Listen then concerning the stones which go into the building. The stones which are square and white and which fit into their joins are the Apostles and bishops and teachers and deacons who walked according to the majesty of God, and served the elect of God in holiness and reverence as bishops and teachers and deacons; some of them are fallen asleep and some are still alive.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage identifies specific occupational roles within the community (bishops, teachers, deacons) as the structural foundation of the group, granting them the resource of religious authority and a primary place in the community's structure.

verbatim: [§85] [4] But who are they who are being brought and placed in the building? They are young in the faith and faithful; but they are being exhorted by the angels to good deeds, because wickedness has been found in them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Individuals classified as 'young in the faith' are subjected to mandatory external exhortation and supervision because of their perceived inherent instability or 'wickedness,' limiting their autonomy compared to the 'square stones' (established leaders).

extracted_rules.rule: Wealthy obligation to feed the poor
verbatim: [§114] Now, therefore, listen to me and be at peace among yourselves, and regard one another and help one another, and do not take a superabundant share of the creatures of God for yourselves, but give also a part to those who lack. [§118] So this lack of sharing is harmful to you who are rich, and do not share with the poor. [§119] Let therefore they who have over-abundance seek out those who are hungry, so long as the tower is not yet finished.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly commands those with 'over-abundance' and the 'rich' to share their food with those who lack it, framing the retention of excess resources as harmful and prohibited by religious instruction in the community.

verbatim: [§120] See to it then, you who rejoice in your wealth, that the destitute may not groan, and their groans go up to the Lord, and you with your goods be shut outside the door of the tower.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a rule where continued focus on wealth at the expense of the destitute results in being 'shut outside' the tower, which represents the saved community/kingdom.

verbatim: [§121] Therefore I speak now to the leaders of the Church and to those who take the chief seats. Be not like the sorcerers, for sorcerers carry their charms in boxes, but you carry your charms and poison in your hearts. [§122] You are hardened, and will not cleanse your hearts, and mix your wisdom together in a pure heart that you may find mercy by the great King.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text addresses internal communal governance, mandating that those in 'chief seats' must cleanse their hearts of 'poison' (malice/discord) to receive mercy, distinguishing their behavioral requirements from regular members.

verbatim: [§140] Why, then, I should like to know, did she sit in a chair, sir? Because every sick person sits in a chair because of his sickness, that the weakness of the body may find support.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a general societal norm or rule: that being sick (the criterion) entitles or necessitates an individual to have the resource of physical support (a chair).

verbatim: [§143] [2] When anyone is old, he already despairs of himself by reason of his weakness and poverty, and expects nothing except the last day of his life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the general social and material condition of the elderly in his time, specifically identifying that old age is associated with a lack of resources and a lack of expectation for material improvement unless an external inheritance occurs.

extracted_rules.rule: Elect dwelling in world to come
extracted_rules.resource: Right to dwell in the world to come
verbatim: [§150] But the white part is the world to come, in which the elect of God shall dwell, for those who have been chosen by God for eternal life will be without spot and pure.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a specific group ('the elect') based on religious criteria who are granted the resource of dwelling in the future world/eternal life, whereas the non-elect are implicitly excluded from this transition from the 'black' world.

verbatim: [§162] You did well to escape it, she said, because you cast your care upon God, and opened your heart to the Lord, believing that salvation can be found through nothing save through the great and glorious name. Therefore the Lord sent his angel, whose name is Thegri, who is over the beast, and shut his mouth that he should not hurt you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a rule where those who belong to the group of believers (specifically those not 'double-minded') receive active protection from destructive forces (symbolized by the beast) through divine intervention.

verbatim: [§190] Do good, and of all your toil which God gives you, give in simplicity to all who need, not doubting to whom you shall give and to whom not: give to all, for to all God wishes gifts to be made of his own bounties. [...] He therefore who gives is innocent; for as he received from the Lord the fulfilment of this ministry, he fulfilled it in simplicity, not doubting to whom he should give or not give.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for those with resources (toil/bounties) to distribute them without the burden of vetting recipients; the moral responsibility/resource of 'innocence' is maintained by the giver regardless of the recipient's merit.

verbatim: [§166] Therefore the Lord sent his angel, whose name is Thegri, who is over the beast, and shut his mouth that he should not hurt you. You have escaped great tribulation through your faith, and because you were not double-minded when you saw so great a beast.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a specific religious rule where faith and single-mindedness grant the group (believers) the resource of protection from external threats (the beast/persecution).

verbatim: [§168] Believe on the Lord, you who are double-minded, that he can do all things, and turns his wrath away from you, and sends scourges on you who are double-minded. Woe to those who hear these words and disobey; it were better for them not to have been born.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific internal religious group ('the double-minded') and specifies they lose the resource of protection from 'scourges' and divine wrath due to their state of mind.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dwell in the world to come
verbatim: [§174] But the white part is the world to come, in which the elect of God shall dwell, for those who have been chosen by God for eternal life will be without spot and pure.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage defines a specific group ('the elect' or 'chosen') and grants them the exclusive resource of habitation in the future world/eternal life.

verbatim: [§190] Those then who receive shall render an account to God why they received it and for what. For those who accepted through distress shall not be punished, but those who accepted in hypocrisy shall pay the penalty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While the distressed poor are protected, the rule explicitly strips the 'hypocritical' poor of the resource of 'exemption from punishment' regarding the receipt of alms.

extracted_rules.rule: Husband's duty to divorce adulterous wife
extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain married to a spouse
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§206] So long as he is ignorant, said he, he does not sin, but if the husband knows her sin, and the wife does not repent, but remains in her fornication, and the husband go on living with her, he becomes a partaker of her sin, and shares in her adultery. What then, said I, sir, shall the husband do if the wife remain in this disposition? Let him put her away, he said, and let the husband remain by himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly commands a husband to divorce (put away) a wife who persists in adultery; continuing the marriage makes him a participant in the sin.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§206] But if he put his wife away and marry another he also commits adultery himself. [...] Therefore, for the sake of repentance the husband ought not to marry.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule mandates that even after a justified divorce due to adultery, the husband may not marry another woman, effectively losing the right to remarry while the option for the first wife's repentance remains.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be received back after adultery
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§206] If then, said I, sir, after the wife be put away she repent, and wish to return to her own husband, shall she not be received? Yes, said he; if the husband do not receive her he sins and covers himself with great sin; but it is necessary to receive the sinner who repents, but not often, for the servants of God have but one repentance.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes that a husband is religiously obligated to receive back a wife who has repented of adultery, meaning she has a right to return to the household.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§206] But I tell you, said he, after that great and holy calling, if a man be tempted by the devil and sin, he has one repentance, but if he sin and repent repeatedly it is unprofitable for such a man, for scarcely shall he live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text restricts the number of times an individual (defined by their 'calling' or conversion) can access the institutional or spiritual resource of 'repentance' for post-baptismal sins to a single occurrence.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from legal/spiritual penalty for receiving aid
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§196] Those then who receive shall render an account to God why they received it and for what. For those who accepted through distress shall not be punished, but those who accepted in hypocrisy shall pay the penalty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Individuals who receive charity due to physical or material distress are granted an exemption from spiritual punishment, whereas those receiving it under false pretenses are penalized.

extracted_rules.resource: Honor and glory with the Lord
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§206] If, sir, said I, a wife, or on the other hand a husband, die, and the survivor marry, does the one who marries commit sin? He does not sin, said he, but if he remain single he gains for himself more exceeding honour and great glory with the Lord, but even if he marry he does not sin.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule specifies that while remarriage is not a sin, remaining single after the death of a spouse confers a higher status of honor and glory.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§212] [5] So long as he is ignorant, said he, he does not sin, but if the husband knows her sin, and the wife does not repent, but remains in her fornication, and the husband go on living with her, he becomes a partaker of her sin, and shares in her adultery. [§213] [6] What then, said I, sir, shall the husband do if the wife remain in this disposition? Let him put her away, he said, and let the husband remain by himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text specifies a rule of conduct for the early Christian community where a husband must expel an unrepentant adulterous wife or become a sharer in her sin.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§216] [9] Not only, said he, is it adultery if a man defile his flesh, but whosoever acts as do the heathen is also guilty of adultery, so that if anyone continue in such practices, and repent not, depart from him and do not live with him, otherwise you are also a sharer in his sin.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mandates social and residential separation from those who persist in non-Christian (heathen) behaviors, characterizing such association as sharing in sin.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§213] [6] Let him put her away, he said, and let the husband remain by himself. But if he put his wife away and marry another he also commits adultery himself. ... [§215] [8] Therefore, for the sake of repentance the husband ought not to marry.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While a husband is required to put away an adulterous wife, he is expressly forbidden from remarrying so as to leave open the possibility of her repentance and return.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§215] [8] Yes, said he; if the husband do not receive her he sins and covers himself with great sin; but it is necessary to receive the sinner who repents, but not often, for the servants of God have but one repentance.
extracted_rules.reasoning: A rule is established that a husband is obligated to take back an unfaithful wife if she repents, though this right is limited to a single occurrence.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§230] [6] But I tell you, said he, after that great and holy calling, if a man be tempted by the devil and sin, he has one repentance, but if he sin and repent repeatedly it is unprofitable for such a man, for scarcely shall he live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: For the community of believers (the 'called'), the author establishes a strict rule that only one opportunity for repentance is available for sins committed after baptism.

extracted_rules.group: those who are filled with faith
verbatim: [§247] [1] Hear, then, said he, the working of ill temper, and how evil it is and how it destroys the servants of God by its working, and how it leads them astray from righteousness. But it does not lead astray those who are filled with faith, nor can it work evil to them, because my power is with them, but it leads astray those who are vain and are double-minded.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule of spiritual immunity where faith acts as a protective barrier. While 'ill temper' leads others astray, this specific group is explicitly stated to be immune to its harmful workings due to divine power being with them.

extracted_rules.group: those who are vain and are double-minded
verbatim: [§247] [1] [...] but it leads astray those who are vain and are double-minded. [§248] [2] And when it sees such men in tranquillity, it forces its way into the heart of that man, and the man or woman is made bitter out of nothing, because of daily business or of food or some trifle...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific category of people ('double-minded') who lack the protection afforded to the fully faithful. They are defined by their spiritual state and are consequently the targets of 'ill temper' which 'forces its way' into them.

verbatim: [§273] Those, therefore who fear him and observe his commandments,—it is they who have life with God. But as for those who do not observe his commandments, neither have they life in him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a clear rule within the early Christian community: those who adhere to the commandments (the in-group of faithful observers) are granted the immaterial resource of eternal or divine life, while those who do not are excluded from it.

verbatim: [§273] But the devil do not fear, for by fearing the Lord you have power over the devil because there is no might in him. But where there is no might, neither is there fear.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text presents a rule of spiritual hierarchy where the group of God-fearers (believers) gains the immaterial resource of 'power' over a spiritual adversary, whereas those without this affiliation are implied to be vulnerable ('despised by all').

extracted_rules.resource: Right to walk the straight path
verbatim: [§255] They relate, then, to the righteous and to the unrighteous: do you therefore believe the righteous, but do not believe the unrighteous. For that which is righteous has a straight path, but that which is unrighteous a crooked path. But do you walk in the straight path, but leave the crooked path alone.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes between two distinct social/spiritual groups (righteous vs unrighteous) based on a religious criterion. The righteous are granted access to a 'straight path' (a metaphor for a smooth, non-harmful life/conduct), while the unrighteous are restricted to a 'crooked path' described as harmful.

verbatim: [§291] [10] Next hear the things which follow: [...] not to oppress poor debtors, and whatever is like to these things. Do you not think, said he, that these things are good? Yes, sir, said I, for what is better than these things? Walk then, said he, in them, and do not refrain from them, and you shall live to God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a specific behavioral norm for the religious community involving the refusal to oppress those in debt, framing this as a mandatory 'good deed' for those who serve God.

verbatim: [§291] [10] Next hear the things which follow: [...] to reverence the aged, to practise justice, to preserve brotherhood, to submit to insult, to be brave, to bear no malice, to comfort those who are oppressed in spirit [...] Walk then, said he, in them, and do not refrain from them, and you shall live to God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author lists 'reverence for the aged' as a mandatory commandment for the community, which constitutes a social resource (status/respect) granted based on the criterion of age.

verbatim: [§291] [10] Next hear the things which follow: To minister to widows, to look after orphans and the destitute, to redeem from distress the servants of God, to be hospitable, for in hospitality may be found the practice of good.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Specific vulnerable groups defined by lineage/family status (widows and orphans) are designated as beneficiaries of 'ministry' and 'care' by the religious group.

verbatim: [§291] [10] Next hear the things which follow: To minister to widows, to look after orphans and the destitute, to redeem from distress the servants of God, to be hospitable, for in hospitality may be found the practice of good.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Wealth/Property status (destitution) is used as the criterion to trigger a mandatory rule of providing material look-after and support from the community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to communal redemption from distress
verbatim: [§291] [10] Next hear the things which follow: [...] to redeem from distress the servants of God, to be hospitable, for in hospitality may be found the practice of good.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Religious identity ('servant of God') allows an individual to access the resource of 'redemption from distress', provided by other members of the faith group.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§300] [5] But if you doubt in your heart, you shall receive none of your petitions. For those who have doubts towards God, these are the double-minded, and they shall not in any wise obtain any of their petitions.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a clear rule within the religious community that those who exhibit the behavioral trait of 'double-mindedness' (doubting) are denied the resource of answered prayer.

extracted_rules.rule: Efficacy of prayer for the faithful
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§301] [6] But they who are perfect, in faith ask for all things, trusting in the Lord, and they receive them, because they ask without doubting, and are double-minded in nothing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly links the group of 'perfect' believers with the successful acquisition of their requests from the deity.

extracted_rules.group: Those mixed up with business and riches
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§313] [4] Listen, he said, those who have never inquired concerning the truth, nor made search concerning the Godhead, but only have faith, and are mixed up with business and riches, and heathen friendships, and many other occupations of this world,—such as are intent on these, do not understand the parables of the Godhead.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies a specific group based on their worldly occupations/occupational status as being barred from the immaterial resource of religious knowledge/intellectual understanding.

extracted_rules.group: Those who have the fear of God
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§315] [6] But they who have the fear of God, and inquire concerning the Godhead and truth, and have their heart towards the Lord, perceive quickly and understand all that is said to them, because they have the fear of the Lord in themselves; for where the Lord dwells, there also is great understanding.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Membership in the group defined by the 'fear of God' (a religious status) grants the resource of quick perception and understanding of religious truths.

extracted_rules.resource: Right of intercession to the altar
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§308] For the intercession of the mournful man has nowhere power to ascend to the altar of God. ... Therefore, the grief which is mixed with his intercession does not permit the intercession to ascend in purity to the altar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a group ('the mournful') who are deprived of the resource of effective spiritual intercession/prayer reaching the divine altar due to their internal state.

verbatim: [§323] For the intercession of the mournful man has nowhere power to ascend to the altar of God. Why, said I, does not the intercession of the mournful man ascend to the altar? Because, said he, grief sits in his heart. Therefore, the grief which is mixed with his intercession does not permit the intercession to ascend in purity to the altar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a spiritual rule where an individual's emotional state (grief/mourning) prevents their prayers or intercessions from effectively reaching God's altar.

verbatim: [§328] In the first place, that man who seems to have a spirit exalts himself... and accepts rewards for his prophecy, and if he does not receive them he does not prophesy. Is it then possible for a Divine Spirit to accept rewards and prophesy? It is not possible for a prophet of God to do this.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines the boundary of the group 'prophets of God' by their inability to accept payment for spiritual services, a rule that distinguishes them from false or earthly prophets.

verbatim: [§328] [H]e who has the spirit which is from above... gives no answers to anyone when he is consulted, nor does he speak by himself (for the Holy Spirit does not speak when a man wishes to speak), but he speaks at that time when God wishes him to speak.
extracted_rules.reasoning: True prophets are subject to a rule where they lack the resource of personal agency over their speech; they are prohibited from answering consultations at will or speaking by their own volition.

verbatim: [§328] He [the false prophet] corrupts the understanding of the double-minded, not of the faithful. Therefore these double-minded men come to him as to a wizard, and ask him concerning their future... But as many as are double-minded, and constantly repent, practise soothsaying, like the heathen, and bring greater shame upon themselves by their idolatry.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text dictates that 'double-minded' individuals are excluded from the guidance of true prophets and instead are relegated to false prophets/soothsayers, which is framed as a loss of truth and a path to idolatry.

verbatim: [§341] [12] In the first place, that man who seems to have a spirit exalts himself and wishes to have the first place... and accepts rewards for his prophecy, and if he does not receive them he does not prophesy. Is it then possible for a Divine Spirit to accept rewards and prophesy? It is not possible for a prophet of God to do this, but the spirit of such prophets is of the earth.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a professional and moral rule for those acting as prophets within the community, explicitly barring true prophets of God from accepting rewards for their services.

verbatim: [§342] [13] Next, on no account does he come near to an assembly of righteous men, but shuns them. But he cleaves to the double-minded and empty, and prophesies to them in a corner, and deceives them by empty speech about everything according to their lusts, for he is also answering the empty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a boundary where false prophets (likened to heathens in previous paragraphs) are excluded or exclude themselves from the formal 'assembly of righteous men,' representing a denial of access to the core community resource.

verbatim: [§333] [4] Therefore, as many as are strong in the faith of the Lord... do not cleave to such spirits... But as many as are double-minded... practise soothsaying, like the heathen, and bring greater shame upon themselves by their idolatry. For he who asks a false prophet concerning any act is an idolator, and empty of the truth and foolish.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While framed religiously, the 'heathen' (non-Christians/foreigners to the faith) are the benchmark for a group lacks the resource of 'truth' and 'salvation' due to their practices, which the author uses to define the boundaries of his own group.

verbatim: [§337] [8] In the first place, he who has the spirit which is from above... gives no answers to anyone when he is consulted, nor does he speak by himself (for the Holy Spirit does not speak when a man wishes to speak), but he speaks at that time when God wishes him to speak.
extracted_rules.reasoning: There is a specific behavioral and professional restriction placed on the 'true prophet' (the religious specialist) regarding when and how they may exercise their primary function.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§368] [6] But now I say to you, if you do not keep them, but neglect them, you shall not have salvation, nor your children, nor your house, because you have already judged for yourself that these commandments cannot be kept by man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where adherence to specific moral commandments by the 'servant of God' (the head of the house) determines the legal/spiritual preservation of the entire household, including children and property (house).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be obeyed by followers
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§365] [3] Fulfil carefully this ministry which I give you, and work much in it, for you will find favour with those who are about to repent, and they will obey your words, for I will be with you, and will force them to be persuaded by you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage outlines a social-religious hierarchy where those assigned a 'ministry' have the specific resource of obedience and persuasion over a defined group (the penitents).

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§381] [4] So also the devil comes to all the servants of God, tempting them; as many therefore as are full of faith withstand him powerfully, and he departs from them, having no room by which to enter. Then, therefore, he comes to those who are half empty and finding room he enters into them, and does what he will in them, and they become his servants.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text divides the community based on the depth of their faith ('full' vs 'empty'). For those with full faith, they gain the resource of self-governance/protection from external spiritual control, whereas the 'empty' lose their autonomy and become 'servants' to another.

extracted_rules.resource: Power to master all things under heaven
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§371] [2] ...do you not understand the glory of God, how great and mighty and wonderful it is, because he created the world for man’s sake, and subdued all his creation to man, and gave him all power, to master all things under heaven?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author defines 'Man' as a group based on their ontological status (Lineage from God/Creation), granting them the resource of political/physical dominion over the natural world.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the city
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§392] [3] For the Lord of this city will say: I do not wish you to dwell in my city, but go out from this city, because you do not use my law. [§393] [4] For the lord of this country justly says to you, Either use my law or go out from my country.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a situation where the governing authority ('the Lord of this city') threatens Christians with expulsion and loss of residency rights if they do not follow the local 'law' (pagan/secular customs), reflecting the precarious status of the religious minority.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain all personal earnings
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§401] The rich man, therefore, helps the poor in all things without doubting. [...] And the rich man likewise provides the poor, without hesitating, with the wealth which he received from the Lord; and this work is great and acceptable with God; because he has understanding in his wealth; and has wrought for the poor man from the gifts of the Lord; and fulfilled his ministry rightly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a social and religious norm where the possession of wealth is tied to a mandatory ministry of redistribution; the wealthy are expected to provide for the poor as a condition of their stewardship of 'the gifts of the Lord'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§401] But when the rich man rests upon the poor, and gives him what he needs, he believes that what he does to the poor man can find a reward with God [...] So also the poor; interceding with the Lord for the rich; complement their wealth; and again; the rich helping the poor with their necessities complement their prayers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage outlines a reciprocal social rule where the poor are granted access to the material resources of the rich in exchange for spiritual intercession, establishing a right to support within the community context.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§397] [8] Therefore instead of lands, purchase afflicted souls, as each is able, and look after widows and orphans, and do not despise them, and spend your wealth and all your establishments for such fields and houses as you have received from God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mandates that members of the community must use their wealth to provide for widows and orphans, groups defined by their lack of family protection, thereby granting them a resource of communal care.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain all personal wealth
verbatim: [§407] But when the rich man rests upon the poor, and gives him what he needs, he believes that what he does to the poor man can find a reward with God, because the poor is rich in intercession and confession, and his intercession has great power with God. The rich man, therefore, helps the poor in all things without doubting.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a religious and social norm in the author's community where the wealthy are expected to distribute their material resources to the poor to ensure their own spiritual standing.

verbatim: [§410] So also the poor; interceding with the Lord for the rich; complement their wealth; and again; the rich helping the poor with their necessities complement their prayers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a social exchange where the group defined by lack of wealth receives material necessities from the wealthy in exchange for spiritual intercession.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive answers to prayer
verbatim: [§424] But abstain from much business, and you will do no sin. For those who do much business also sin much, being engrossed in their business, and serving their Lord in nothing. [§425] How then, said he, can such a one pray for anything from the Lord and receive it, when he does not serve the Lord? They who serve him,—they shall receive their requests. But they who do not serve the Lord,—they shall receive nothing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author establishes a rule that those heavily occupied with business (an occupation-based group) are effectively barred from the resource of successful divine petition (receiving what they pray for).

verbatim: [§428] And he chose out a certain servant, who was faithful, in good esteem and honour with him, and he called him and said to him: Take this vineyard which I have planted, and fence it until I come, and do nothing more to the vineyard. And follow this order of mine and you shall have your freedom from me.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a common social practice where a master promises freedom (manumission) to a slave in exchange for following specific orders and providing faithful service.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to communal food and financial support
verbatim: [§428] And you shall do thus: After completing what has been written, in that day on which you fast you shall taste nothing except bread and water, and you shall reckon the price of the expense for that day which you are going to keep, of the foods which you would have eaten, and you shall give it to a widow or an orphan or to some one destitute, and you shall thus be humble-minded that through your humility he who receives it may fill his soul and pray to the Lord for you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mandates a specific redistribution of wealth where the money saved from fasting is legally/normatively directed to specific vulnerable groups (widows, orphans, the destitute).

verbatim: [§428] So in reward for this work which he has done I wish to make him joint heir with my son, because, when he had a good thought he did not put it on one side, but carried it out. The son of the master agreed with this plan, that the servant should be joint heir with the son.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Through the parable used to explain contemporary religious concepts, the author reflects a social reality where a master can elevate a slave to the status of a joint-heir alongside a natural son as a reward for exceptional service.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive food from master's table
verbatim: [§428] After a few days he made a feast and sent to him much food from the feast. But the servant took the food which was sent to him by the master, kept what was sufficient for himself, and distributed the rest to his fellow-servants.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text illustrates a social convention where masters share leftovers or gifts from feasts with their slaves, which then the head slave further distributes to fellow-servants.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§435] And he chose out a certain servant... and he called him and said to him: Take this vineyard which I have planted, and fence it until I come, and do nothing more to the vineyard. And follow this order of mine and you shall have your freedom from me.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal/social expectation within the Roman context where a master promises freedom (manumission) to a servant as a reward for following specific instructions.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§442] And he said to them: I promised this servant his freedom if he kept the orders which I gave him. Now he has kept my orders, and has added good work in the vineyard, and greatly pleased me. So in reward for this work which he has done I wish to make him joint heir with my son.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The master rewards the servant's exceptional initiative (exceeding orders) by elevating him to the status of a joint heir alongside the biological son, showing that a freed or favored slave could be granted inheritance rights.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§454] After completing what has been written, in that day on which you fast you shall taste nothing except bread and water, and you shall reckon the price of the expense for that day... and you shall give it to a widow or an orphan or to some one destitute, and you shall thus be humble-minded that through your humility he who receives it may fill his soul and pray to the Lord for you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author outlines a specific communal rule for the early Christian community: the financial savings from fasting must be redistributed to vulnerable groups (widows, orphans, destitute), establishing their right to charity.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§460] Whoever, said he, is God’s servant, and has his Lord in his heart, seeks understanding from him and receives it, and he interprets every parable, and the sayings of the Lord which were spoken through parables are made known to him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule for access to spiritual knowledge (immaterial resource); 'servants' (meaning dedicated believers/religious figures) are granted the right to understand and interpret complex parables that remain hidden from others.

extracted_rules.rule: Subjugation of the Son of God
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§469] Listen, said he: The Son of God is not given the form of a servant., but is given great power and lordship... Because God planted the vineyard, said he, that is, created the people, and gave it over to his Son.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text discusses the ontological status of the Son of God, contrasting the appearance of a 'servant' (slave) with the reality of 'lordship' over the people. It defines the formal status of the divine figure in relation to the human 'vineyard'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§469] For all flesh in which the Holy Spirit has dwelt shall receive a reward if it be found undefiled and spotless. ... [§476] that this flesh also, having served the Spirit blamelessly, should have some place of sojourn, and not seem to have lost the reward of its service.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where individuals (defined here as 'flesh' inhabited by the Spirit) who maintain purity are granted a specific resource: a 'place of sojourn' or 'reward'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§478] For if you defile your flesh you defile also the Holy Spirit, and if you defile the flesh you shall not live. ... [§480] For if you defile your flesh you defile also the Holy Spirit, and if you defile the flesh you shall not live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author establishes a negative condition: defiling the flesh/spirit results in the loss of 'life' (spiritual/eternal life), which is a core immaterial resource in this community.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§483] But the sheep which you see not skipping, but feeding in one place, these are they who have given themselves up to luxury and deceit, but have uttered no blasphemy against the Lord. These then have been corrupted from the truth; in them there is hope of repentance, in which they can live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text distinguishes between two groups of sinners based on whether they blasphemed. Those who did not blaspheme are granted the resource of 'hope of repentance'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§483] For these, then, there is no repentance of life, because they added to their sins and blasphemed against the name of God. Such men incur death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Blasphemy acts as a criterion for excluding a group from the possibility of repentance and life, leading to 'death'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§483] And if they repent... they glorify the Lord that they were handed over to me, and they no longer suffer any of the evils.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Once a member of the group 'sinners' repents, they gain protection from the 'evils' (punishments) administered by the angel of punishment.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§494] [3] Listen, he said, the sheep which you see joyful and skipping, these are those which have been torn away from God completely, and have given themselves up to the lusts of this world. For these, then, there is no repentance of life, because they added to their sins and blasphemed against the name of God. Such men incur death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a specific religious rule where a subgroup of sinners (those who blaspheme) are permanently excluded from the possibility of repentance, leading to 'death' rather than the 'corruption' which allows for renewal.

extracted_rules.rule: Right to repent for non-blasphemous sinners
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§495] [4] But the sheep which you see not skipping, but feeding in one place, these are they who have given themselves up to luxury and deceit, but have uttered no blasphemy against the Lord. These then have been corrupted from the truth; in them there is hope of repentance, in which they can live.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage differentiates between two types of religious offenders, granting the 'resource' of hope for repentance specifically to those who did not commit blasphemy.

extracted_rules.rule: Protection from worldly loss and illness
extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal and material afflictions
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§503] [4] Hear, said he, the different tortures and punishments. The tortures befall them in this life, for some are punished with loss, others with deprivations, others with divers illnesses, others with all unsettlement, and others are insulted by the unworthy, and suffer many other things.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic rule of punishment where individuals who 'wander away from God' lose access to material stability and health as a form of divine correction/torture.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§510] [4] If, therefore, any man live in luxury and deceit for one day, and be punished one day, the day of punishment has the power of a whole year, for a man is punished as many years as he has lived days in luxury.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This establishes a mathematical rule for the duration of a loss of freedom (punishment). The resource 'Freedom from punishment' is reduced in a specific ratio connected to the duration of the sin.

verbatim: [§523] [3] I said to him: Sir, even if they have done such things that the glorious angel is enraged, what have I done? They cannot, said he, be punished in any other way, than if you, the head of the house, be afflicted. For when you are afflicted, they also will necessarily be afflicted, but while you prosper, they cannot suffer any affliction.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a social and spiritual rule where the head of the household is physically or mentally afflicted as a mechanism to punish or refine the entire family unit; the resource of protection from others' misdeeds is denied to the head.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from physical or mental affliction
verbatim: [§524] [4] ...do you then think that the sins of those who repent are immediately forgiven? By no means; but he who repents must torture his own soul, and be humble in all his deeds and be afflicted with many divers afflictions.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a rule of the early Christian community where repentance does not grant immediate relief but requires the individual to undergo specific afflictions and self-torture.

verbatim: [§528] Who then, Sir, said I, are they who were crowned and went into the tower? All those, said he, who wrestled with the devil and conquered him, have been crowned. These are they who suffered for the law.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a specific reward—the crown of palm leaves—for those who physically suffered for their religious adherence, distinguishing them from other believers.

verbatim: [§528] Listen, said he, those whose sticks are dry and were found moth-eaten are the apostates and the betrayers of the Church, and blasphemers of the Lord in their sins; and moreover they were ashamed of the name of the Lord which was called over them. These then have finally perished to God. And you see that not even one of them repented... from such life is departed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a group defined by religious betrayal and explicitly states that for them, the possibility of life (salvation) and the gift of repentance are absent.

verbatim: [§528] But those who gave up their sticks half-green and half-dry these are those who are concerned with business and do not cleave to the saints; for this reason half of them is alive, and half is dead.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the pursuit of business as a barrier that prevents individuals from fully associating (cleaving) with the religious community (the saints).

verbatim: [§528] ...these are they who were faithful, but became rich and in honour among the heathen; then they put on great haughtiness and became high-minded, and abandoned the truth, and did not cleave to the righteous, but lived together with the heathen... remained in the faith, without doing the works of the faith.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Acquiring wealth is presented as a mechanism that removes the individual from the community of the righteous and causes them to lose the resource of active faith practice.

verbatim: [§528] But in whom he saw guile and wickedness, that they would repent with hypocrisy, to them he gave no repentance, lest they should again defile his name.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Repentance is a resource provided by the shepherd/angel, but it is explicitly withheld from those judged to be hypocritical to prevent and protect against further religious defilement.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to seals and white clothing
verbatim: [§528] And he sent also the others into the tower who gave up their sticks green and with buds; but the buds without fruit; and he gave them seals. And all who went into the tower had the same clothing; white as snow.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Specific religious markers (seals and white clothing) are given to groups who maintained their green sticks (faith) during persecution or holy living.

verbatim: [§549] [1] And the angel of the Lord commanded crowns to be brought; and crowns were brought; made; as it were; of palm leaves; and he crowned the men who had given up their sticks with buds and some fruit; and sent them away into the tower.

[§564] [6] Who then, Sir, said I, are they who were crowned and went into the tower? All those, said he, who wrestled with the devil and conquered him, have been crowned. These are they who suffered for the law.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where a specific subset of the religious community (those who 'suffered' or 'wrestled with the devil') are granted the specific resource of being crowned, a distinction not given to all believers.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dwell in the tower
verbatim: [§550] [2] And he sent also the others into the tower who gave up their sticks green and with buds; but the buds without fruit; and he gave them seals.
[§565] [7] And the others who also gave up their sticks green and having buds, but without fruit, are they who were persecuted for the law, but did not suffer, and did not deny their law.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Those who faced persecution without 'suffering' (likely meaning martyrdom) but remained faithful are granted access to the 'tower' and receive seals, distinguishing their status within the group.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dwell in the tower
verbatim: [§552] [4] And he sent away those who had given up their sticks green, as they had received them, and gave them clothing and seals.
[§566] [8] And those who gave them up green, as they received them, are holy and righteous and have walked far with a pure heart, and have kept the commandments of the Lord.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Believers who maintained their purity and kept the commandments (symbolized by green sticks) are granted the resource of specific clothing, seals, and entrance to the tower.

verbatim: [§563] [5] I said to him: Sir, why did he send some into the tower, and left some to you? All those who have transgressed against the law, which they received from him, he left to my authority for repentance. But as many as were already well-pleased with the law, and have observed it, he keeps them under his own authority.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Individuals who have transgressed the law are removed from the 'authority' of the Son/Angel and placed under the 'authority for repentance', losing the status of being 'kept' directly by divine authority until they repent.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to cleave to the saints
verbatim: [§595] But those who gave up their sticks half-green and half-dry these are those who are concerned with business and do not cleave to the saints; for this reason half of them is alive, and half is dead.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies a specific social group ('those concerned with business') and notes a systematic lack of spiritual/communal association ('do not cleave to the saints') as a result of their occupation.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dwell in the tower
verbatim: [§593] But if any of them turn again to schism he shall be cast out from the tower, and shall lose his life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes a rule where members who engage in schism (a religious/behavioral boundary) lose their right to reside in the 'tower' (the primary communal/spiritual collective).

verbatim: [§581] Listen, said he, those whose sticks are dry and were found moth-eaten are the apostates and the betrayers of the Church, and blasphemers of the Lord in their sins... These then have finally perished to God. And you see that not even one of them repented, although they heard the words which you spoke to them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Apostasy is treated as a near-immutable religious group status in this context; the text explicitly states that for this group, access to the mechanism of repentance is closed or non-functioning.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain in the church
verbatim: [§581] And they who gave up their sticks dry and not moth-eaten, these are also near them; for they were hypocrites, and introduced strange doctrines and corrupted the servants of God... as many as shall not repent have lost their lives.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Those characterized by a specific intellectual/educational output (strange doctrines) are categorized as a group whose existence in the community is under threat of expiration (death/loss of life) unless they change their status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to cleave to the saints
verbatim: [§596] [1] But those who gave up their sticks half-green and half-dry these are those who are concerned with business and do not cleave to the saints; for this reason half of them is alive, and half is dead.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies business concern as a specific occupation-based barrier that prevents individuals from fully participating in the community of 'saints'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to cleave to the righteous
verbatim: [§601] And those who gave up their sticks two-thirds dry, and one-third green, these are they who were faithful, but became rich and in honour among the heathen; then they put on great haughtiness and became high-minded, and abandoned the truth, and did not cleave to the righteous, but lived together with the heathen, and this way pleased them better.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that achieving wealth and honor among the 'heathen' leads to a separation from the group defined as 'the righteous'.

verbatim: [§604] [3] But others lived to the end with the heathen, and were corrupted by the vainglory of the heathen, and were apostates from God, and did the deeds of the heathen. These were reckoned with the heathen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule where those who prioritize wealth and status (vainglory) among the heathens are legally or formally 'reckoned' as members of that out-group, losing their standing in the original group.

verbatim: [§597] [2] Many, then, of them, when they heard my commandments repented. As many, as repented, have their dwelling in the tower; but some of them were apostate to the end. These then have no repentance, for because of their business they blasphemed the Lord and denied him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines apostates (those who deny the Lord) as a group that is barred from 'repentance', which is the mechanism for re-entering the 'tower' (the community).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be received into houses
verbatim: [§606] ...but they were never apostates from God, and they bore the name gladly, and they gladly received into their houses the servants of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage highlights a societal rule/norm within the group that 'servants of God' have the right to be received into the homes of those who bear the name of the faith.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the Kingdom of God
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§617] Of them are those who are rich and are mixed up with many affairs of business, for the thistles are the rich, and the thorns are those who are mixed up with various affairs of business. These then who are engaged in many and various businesses do not cleave to the servants of God, but are choked by their work and go astray. And the rich cleave with difficulty to the servants of God, fearing that they will be asked for something by them. Such then (will enter with difficulty the kingdom of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes how the possession of riches and the preoccupation with business affairs prevent individuals from associating with religious leaders and make their entry into the spiritual kingdom difficult.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§617] All of you, then, as many as shall continue, said he, and shall be as babes, with no wickedness, shall be more glorious than all those who have been mentioned before, for all babes are glorious before God, and are in the first place by him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where the 'innocent babe' (representing both literal infants and those who emulate them) holds the 'first place' in the divine hierarchy, gaining more spiritual glory than other groups.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§617] From the ninth mountain, which was desert... are such believers as these: Those with spots are ministers who ministered amiss, and devoured the living of widows and orphans, and made gain for themselves from the ministry which they had received to administer. If then they remain in the same covetousness they are dead and they have no hope of life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific professional group (ministers/deacons) and restricts their right to profit from their office, specifically regarding the property of vulnerable groups like widows.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§617] And from the tenth mountain, where were trees sheltering some sheep, are such believers as these: Bishops and hospitable men who at all times received the servants of God into their houses gladly and without hypocrisy; and the bishops ever ceaselessly sheltered the destitute and the widows by their ministration, and ever behaved with holiness. These then shall all be always sheltered by the Lord.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Bishops who fulfill their social and professional duties (hospitality and care for the destitute) are granted a specific resource of divine protection/shelter.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§617] From the first mountain, the black one, are such believers as these: apostates and blasphemers against the Lord, and betrayers of the servants of God. For these there is no repentance, but there is death, for their race is lawless.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly denies the resource of 'repentance' (a legal/spiritual pathway to reintegration) to the group defined by the act of apostasy.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§617] Their riches have hidden them a little from the truth and darkened them... he commanded their wealth to be cut down, yet not to be wholly taken away from them, that they may be able to do some good with that which was left them, and they shall live to God because they are of a good kind. Therefore they were cut down a little, and placed in the building of this tower.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule where wealthy individuals must have their property 'cut down' (partially confiscated/reassigned) to make them 'meet' for the community/kingdom.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to facilitate entry into the building
verbatim: [§643] You must on no account put stones into the building, but put them by the side of the tower, that the maidens may bring them in through the gate, and give them over for the building. For if, said they, they are not brought in by the hands of these maidens through the gate they cannot change their colours; do not then, said they, labour in vain.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a specific functional rule for the construction of the tower (representing the Church) where only the maidens have the authority to bring building materials through the gate to ensure they are valid for the structure. This grants women (in this symbolic visionary role) a specific religious-legal monopoly over the process of inclusion.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to bring stones through the gate
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§651] [8] And they said to the men who were bringing the stones in: You must on no account put stones into the building, but put them by the side of the tower, that the maidens may bring them in through the gate, and give them over for the building. For if, said they, they are not brought in by the hands of these maidens through the gate they cannot change their colours.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a specific procedural rule for the construction of the tower where only the maidens are permitted to carry the building materials through the gate to ensure they transform correctly. Men are explicitly barred from this specific action.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to bring stones through the gate
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§651] [8] And they said to the men who were bringing the stones in: You must on no account put stones into the building, but put them by the side of the tower, that the maidens may bring them in through the gate, and give them over for the building.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage explicitly forbids the men (the stone-bringers) from putting the stones into the building themselves, requiring them to leave the stones outside for the maidens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain at the tower during rest
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§653] [1] And the six men commanded all the builders to retire a little and rest, but they commanded the maidens not to go away from the tower. [...] [§672] [3] But the maidens stood round the tower guarding it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: During the construction pause, a distinction is made between the 'builders' (who are commanded to leave and rest) and the 'maidens' (who are commanded to stay and guard the structure).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain at the tower during rest
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§653] [1] And the six men commanded all the builders to retire a little and rest, but they commanded the maidens not to go away from the tower.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The builders are formally commanded to withdraw from the site of the resource (the tower) during the interval, unlike the maidens who are required to stay.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform construction and maintenance
verbatim: [§695] [2] ...So he filled up the marks of the stones which had gone into the building, and commanded all round the tower to be swept, and be made clean. [§696] [3] And the maidens took brooms and swept, and they took away all the dirt from the tower and sprinkled water, and the place of the tower became joyful and very beautiful.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a regular labor division where 'maidens' (young women) are the ones specifically commanded and empowered to handle the final structural fitting and the purification/cleaning of the tower site.

verbatim: [§687] [2] I, Sir, said I, have not this art, I am neither a stone-cutter, nor can I understand. Do you not see, said he, that they are very round, and if I wish to make them square, a great deal must be cut away from them?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The narrator explicitly links his lack of understanding regarding the resource (the tower building process) to his lack of specific occupational training as a stone-cutter, establishing a boundary between skilled laborers and those without the 'art'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold custody over others
verbatim: [§699] [6] ...The shepherd called them and said to them: I entrust him to you till I come, and he went away. [§703] [2] ...But they answered and said to me: You were given to our charge; you cannot go away from us.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule of custody where a group of women ('maidens') are granted legal/moral authority over a male individual. This gives them the resource of physical control and the right to prevent his departure.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to the Kingdom of God
verbatim: [§711] So, said he, no man shall enter into the Kingdom of God, except he take his holy name. For if you wish to enter into a city, and that city has been walled round, and has one gate, can you enter into that city except through the gate which it has? No, Sir, said I, for how is it possible otherwise? If then you are not able to enter into the city except through the gate which it has, so, said he, a man cannot, otherwise enter into the kingdom of God, except through the name of his Son, who was beloved by him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a strict communal boundary based on religious affiliation (bearing the 'name'). It uses the metaphor of a walled city with a single gate to describe the exclusion of those who do not possess the specific religious identifier from the 'Kingdom of God'.

verbatim: [§711] The glorious man, said he, is the Son of God, and those six are glorious angels supporting him on the right hand and on the left. None of these glorious angels, said he, can enter into God’s presence without him. Whoever receives not his name shall not enter into the kingdom of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Even high-ranking spiritual groups (angels) are described as having restricted access to the central authority/presence of God unless they act through or in the name of the 'Son', indicating a hierarchy of access within this spiritual community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain in the Church
verbatim: [§720] After some time, then, they were made disobedient by the women whom you saw clothed in black raiment, who had their shoulders bare, and their hair loose, and were beautiful. When they saw them they desired them, and put on their power, and put off the clothing and power of the maidens. They were therefore rejected from the house of God and were handed over to those women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule of expulsion from the community ('the house of God' or Church) for those who, despite initially being members, adopt the behaviors/norms of an out-group. This demonstrates a loss of the resource of membership due to a shift in behavioral alignment.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the Kingdom of God
verbatim: [§746] [2] They had need, said he, to come up through the water that they might be made alive, for they could not, otherwise enter into the kingdom of God, unless they put away the mortality of their former life. [§747] [3] So these also who had fallen asleep received the seal of the Son of God and entered into the kingdom of God. [...] [§748] [4] The seal, then, is the water. They go down then into the water dead, and come up alive.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a general religious rule where the 'seal' (identified as the water of baptism) is a mandatory requirement for any person to transition from 'dead' to 'alive' and gain access to the Kingdom of God.

verbatim: [§760] [2] For this reason, those who have no knowledge of God and do wickedly, are condemned to death, but those who have knowledge of God and have seen his great deeds, and do wickedly, shall be punished doubly, and shall die for ever.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule of differential sentencing based on the level of religious education/knowledge. While those ignorant of God are condemned to death, those with 'knowledge' are explicitly subject to 'double' punishment and eternal death for the same wicked actions.

extracted_rules.rule: Exclusion of the defiled from Church
extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain in the Church
verbatim: [§757] [5] But after they entered in together and became one body, some of them defiled themselves and were cast out from the family of the righteous, and became again what they had been before, or rather even worse. [...] [§761] [3] ...so the Church of God also shall be, after it has been purified, and the wicked and hypocrites and blasphemers and double-minded, and doers of various wickedness, have been rejected from it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage outlines the rule for maintaining the 'purity' of the religious community (the Church) by the systematic rejection and casting out of members who exhibit specific behaviors or internal states (hypocrisy, blasphemy).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to enter the Kingdom of God
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§768] Of them are those who are rich and are mixed up with many affairs of business, for the thistles are the rich, and the thorns are those who are mixed up with various affairs of business. These then who are engaged in many and various businesses do not cleave to the servants of God, but are choked by their work and go astray. [...] [§771] For just as it is difficult to walk with naked feet among thistles, so it is also difficult, for such men to enter into the Kingdom of God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies individuals engaged in 'many and various businesses' as a distinct social group defined by their occupation. It specifies that this group experiences difficulty in entering the Kingdom due to being 'choked by their work'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§782] And for the rest of such men repentance is waiting, for they were not wicked, but rather foolish and without understanding. If therefore these repent they will live to God, but if they do not repent they will dwell with the women who devise evil against them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes a group based on their lack of 'understanding' or 'education' (foolishness) and explicitly grants them the resource of repentance because their errors were based on lack of knowledge rather than malice.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§789] They were ever simple and guileless and blessed and had nothing against one another, but ever rejoiced in the servants of God [...] [§793] And I, the angel of repentance, say to you who are such:— Remain such as you are, and your seed shall not be blotted out for ever, for the Lord has proved you and written you in among our number.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text promises the protection of 'seed' (lineage) as a specific resource for a group defined by their simple and guileless nature, contrasting them with others whose memory or lineage might be destroyed.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to association with angels after death
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§794] Apostles and teachers who preached to all the world, and taught reverently and purely the word of the Lord [...] [§796] The passing of such is with the angels.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a professional/functional group (Apostles and teachers) and grants them a specific immaterial resource (passing with the angels) based on the performance of their office.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from the burden of hospitality
