extracted_rules.verbatim: [§808] Bishops and hospitable men who at all times received the servants of God into their houses gladly and without hypocrisy; and the bishops ever ceaselessly sheltered the destitute and the widows by their ministration, and ever behaved with holiness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the normative behavior of bishops within the contemporary Christian community, specifically their duty to provide material shelter and ministration to the destitute and widows.

extracted_rules.resource: Priority of status in the kingdom
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§822] All of you, then, as many as shall continue, said he, and shall be as babes, with no wickedness, shall be more glorious than all those who have been mentioned before, for all babes are glorious before God, and are in the first place by him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While used metaphorically for believers, the rule explicitly anchors the group 'babes' as a near-immutable age category that naturally receives 'the first place' and 'more glory' before God compared to other categories.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§829] When therefore the Lord saw their minds, that they are able to favour the truth and to remain good, he commanded their wealth to be cut down, yet not to be wholly taken away from them, that they may be able to do some good with that which was left them, and they shall live to God because they are of a good kind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific rule for wealthy believers where their riches are 'cut down' (partially confiscated or restricted) by divine/communal mandate to ensure they remain virtuous while still having enough to do 'some good'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§818] If the heathen punish their servants, if one deny his lord, what think you will the Lord, who has power over all, do to you?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author references a contemporary social fact: non-Christian (heathen) masters have the right to punish their servants (slaves) for acts of denial or disobedience.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the kingdom of God
verbatim: [§830] But this world and the vanities of their riches must be cut away from them, and then they will be meet for the kingdom of God. For they needs must enter into the kingdom of God; for the Lord blessed this innocent kind.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that 'riches must be cut away' before individuals are considered 'meet' (fitting) for the kingdom of God, indicating that the possession of wealth is an obstacle to this spiritual resource.

verbatim: [§836] But if the shepherds themselves be found fallen away, what shall they answer to the Master of the flock? That they have fallen away because of the sheep? They will not be believed, for it is incredible that a shepherd should be harmed by the sheep, and they will rather be punished for their lie.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The 'shepherds' (leaders/clergy) are held to a strict standard of accountability where they are denied the defense of being led astray by their subordinates and are specifically marked for punishment if they fail.

verbatim: [§833] I, the angel of repentance, judge you all happy who are innocent as babes, for your part is good and honourable with God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Using the category of 'babes' as the standard for innocence, the text assigns a high status ('good and honourable with God') to those who embody this age-based characteristic.

verbatim: [§847] But I say that every man ought to be taken out from distress, for he who is destitute and suffers distress in his daily life is in great anguish and necessity. Whoever therefore rescues the soul of such a man from necessity gains great joy for himself. [...] Whoever therefore knows the distress of such a man, and does not rescue him, incurs great sin and becomes guilty of his blood.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule of mandatory assistance for the 'destitute', framing it as a sin and a blood-guilt for others if they fail to provide this rescue.

verbatim: [§863] But I say that every man ought to be taken out from distress, for he who is destitute and suffers distress in his daily life is in great anguish and necessity. Whoever therefore rescues the soul of such a man from necessity gains great joy for himself. [...] Whoever therefore knows the distress of such a man, and does not rescue him, incurs great sin and becomes guilty of his blood.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a communal obligation to provide material or spiritual rescue to those who are 'destitute,' framing it as a rule where the vulnerable are entitled to be 'taken out from distress' by those who know of their condition.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in the Church (the tower)
verbatim: [§867] Therefore do good deeds, all you who have learnt of the Lord, lest the building of the tower be finished while you delay to do them. For the work of the building has been broken off for your sake. Unless therefore you hasten to do right the tower will be finished and you will be shut out.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific resource (being part of the 'tower,' a metaphor for the Church/salvation) available to those who have 'learnt of the Lord' and perform 'good deeds.' It establishes the risk of exclusion ('shut out') for those who fail these behavioral/religious requirements.
